one_line_takeaway: APA makes exact, GDPR-style unlearning practical for LoRA-based LLM recommenders by retraining only the affected shard's adapter and merging adapters into a single one via sample-adaptive validation-error weighting -- delivering ~K-fold faster unlearning and single-pass inference with little-to-no accuracy loss, though its privacy/removal guarantee is argued structurally rather than measured against any adversary.
key_finding: APA preserves recommendation accuracy far better than shard-based baselines while achieving ~K-fold unlearning speedup and near-single-model inference. On Book, APA even exceeds full Retraining (APA-ND AUC 0.6846, Delta +0.0108; APA-D 0.6829, Delta +0.0091) whereas SISA/RecEraser/GraphEraser all fall slightly below Retraining (0.6738). On Movie the gap is larger and APA does NOT beat Retraining (0.7428) but loses far less than baselines: APA-D 0.7259 (Delta -0.0169) and APA-ND 0.7256 (Delta -0.0172) vs SISA -0.0393, RecEraser -0.0491, GraphEraser -0.0525. Efficiency: on Movie, APA unlearning took 10,335 s, ~3.96x faster than Retraining (approx equal to K=4). Inference: aggregated single-pass APA adds only ~0.02 s/sample over a single model, while SISA (K-pass prediction-level aggregation) is much slower. Ablations show the semantic partition matters (Book: 0.6829 vs Random 0.6503 / User 0.6627 / BEKM 0.6411) and validation-error weighting beats average/major/semantic weighting; performance is stable from shard size 512 to 256 and only drops at 128.
core_contribution: Introduces APA (Adapter Partition and Aggregation), presented as the first exact-unlearning framework tailored to LLM-based recommendation (LLMRec). Its genuine novelty is NOT the exact-removal guarantee (which is inherited from retraining-on-disjoint-shards, the same mechanism as SISA), but the aggregation design that makes shard-based unlearning viable for costly LLM inference: (1) a balanced, semantic-aware K-means partition that clusters training instructions by the base LLM's hidden representations into disjoint shards; (2) parameter-level LoRA adapter aggregation at either decomposition level (averaging the A and B low-rank matrices, APA-D) or non-decomposition level (averaging the BA product, APA-ND), so that K sub-adapters collapse into ONE adapter enabling single-pass inference instead of K forward passes; and (3) a sample-adaptive, training-free attention weighting (Eq. 7-8) that assigns each test sample's aggregation weights via a softmax over each sub-adapter's prediction errors on the top-n most similar validation samples (temperature tau). To unlearn a data point, only the single sub-adapter whose shard contained it is retrained.
threat_model: This is a privacy / right-to-be-forgotten paper (CCS concept: Security and privacy -> Privacy protections), not an adversarial-attack paper. The motivating risk: recommendation instruction data (users' historical behaviors) fine-tuned into an LLM can be leaked/extracted from the model (the paper cites Carlini et al. training-data extraction and related LLM leakage work), and regulations such as GDPR require timely, COMPLETE removal of a user's data on request. The specific weakness APA targets is that prior LLM-unlearning methods are APPROXIMATE (e.g. Who's Harry Potter [12], In-Context Unlearning [25], gradient-ascent unlearning [35]) and therefore risk incomplete erasure, leaving residual data recoverable. Important honesty caveat: the paper does NOT empirically measure removal completeness or run any membership-inference / extraction adversary. Following RecEraser, it argues all compared methods are retraining-based and thus 'inherently achieve complete removal' (Sec 4.2) and explicitly omits that evaluation. So the privacy guarantee is asserted structurally, not demonstrated against an attacker.
limitations: (1) Removal completeness is assumed from the retraining structure and never verified empirically -- no membership-inference or extraction attack, so the privacy claim central to the motivation is untested. (2) Unlearning is evaluated only under the streaming single-request assumption (|D_r| = 1); batch unlearning is deferred to future work. (3) Small scale: training set capped at 1024 samples, only K=4 shards, two datasets (BookCrossing, MovieLens-100K), and one backbone (TALLRec) -- efficiency gains near K may not generalize when many shards are hit or data is large. (4) Method is specific to LoRA / adapter-style PEFT; not applicable to full fine-tuning. (5) The gold-standard Retraining still wins on Movie, so 'can even improve over retraining' holds only on Book. (6) Manuscript is a preprint using a placeholder ACM template (Conference'17, placeholder author block), i.e. not peer-reviewed in this form.

datasets_used: BookCrossing (Book): user ratings 1-10 binarized at threshold 5 into like/dislike; book title/author text attributes
MovieLens-100K (Movie): ratings 1-5, >3 = like else dislike; movie title/director text attributes
Per-dataset splits: train capped at 1024, validation 500, test 1000 (TALLRec preprocessing)
quantitative_results: Book AUC -- Retraining 0.6738; SISA 0.6728 (Delta -0.001); GraphEraser 0.6684 (Delta -0.0052); RecEraser 0.6732 (Delta -0.0006); APA-D 0.6829 (Delta +0.0091); APA-ND 0.6846 (Delta +0.0108)
Book Bef.Agg (avg sub-model AUC) -- SISA 0.6570; GraphEraser 0.6443; RecEraser 0.6620; APA 0.6578
Movie AUC -- Retraining 0.7428; SISA 0.7035 (Delta -0.0393); GraphEraser 0.6903 (Delta -0.0525); RecEraser 0.6937 (Delta -0.0491); APA-D 0.7259 (Delta -0.0169); APA-ND 0.7256 (Delta -0.0172)
Movie Bef.Agg (avg sub-model AUC) -- SISA 0.7003; GraphEraser 0.6672; RecEraser 0.6712; APA 0.6696
Unlearning efficiency (Movie): APA 10,335 s, ~3.96x faster than full Retraining (approx K=4)
Inference: APA single-pass aggregated adapter adds ~0.02 s/sample vs one model; SISA (K-pass) much higher
Partition ablation (Book, semantic vs alternatives): Ours 0.6829 vs Random 0.6503, User-based 0.6627, BEKM 0.6411
Config: shard size 256 -> K=4; tau=1000; |N_v|=20 (Movie)/100 (Book); NVIDIA A40 GPUs; shard-size sweep {512,256,128} stable 512->256, drops at 128
baselines_compared: Retraining from scratch (gold standard, full model retrained excluding unusable data)
RecEraser (recommendation-specific, user-based partition UBP; SISA-style aggregation in this adaptation)
GraphEraser (BEKM graph-partition; SISA-style aggregation in this adaptation)
